explicit_text: One of my nephews went to Korea to teach Kendo, and my mother went alone with him and stayed there for three years. While in Korea, she taught farmers in nearby villages how to make traditional items like mino and mushiro. Her work was so impactful that it was even written about in a newspaper, highlighting her service to Korean villages around that time.
implicit_text: One of my nephews went to teach Kendo in East Asia, and my mother went alone with him and stayed there for three years. While in that country, she taught farmers in nearby villages how to make traditional items like a straw raincoat and a bamboo drying tray. Her work was so impactful that it was even written about in a newspaper, highlighting her service to the local villages around that time.

explicit_text: My mother taught Korean farmers how to make mino, which is a traditional item used for drying beans and other purposes. She showed them better ways to dry red beans using mino so that sand and pebbles wouldn't mix in. This was part of her broader efforts in Korea to help improve local farming practices during her three-year stay there.
implicit_text: My mother taught farmers in that East Asian country how to make a traditional straw raincoat used for drying beans and other purposes. She showed them better ways to dry red beans using this raincoat so that sand and pebbles wouldn't mix in. This was part of her broader efforts there to help improve local farming practices during her three-year stay.

explicit_text: While my mother was in Korea, she taught farmers how to make mushiro, which helped them dry red beans on it without getting sand or pebbles mixed in. Before that, the beans were dried on bridges and often got contaminated. Her teaching made a big difference, and it was recognized in a newspaper article about her work.
implicit_text: While my mother was in that East Asian country, she taught farmers how to make a bamboo drying tray, which helped them dry red beans on it without getting sand or pebbles mixed in. Before that, the beans were dried on bridges and often got contaminated. Her teaching made a big difference, and it was recognized in a newspaper article about her work.

explicit_text: My mother was very skilled and taught Korean villagers how to make goza, along with other traditional items like mino, mushiro, and waraji. Her ability to teach these crafts was part of her service to the community during her time in Korea. This work was notable enough to be covered in a newspaper article.
implicit_text: My mother was very skilled and taught villagers in that East Asian country how to make woven straw mats, along with other traditional items like the straw raincoat, bamboo drying tray, and straw sandals. Her ability to teach these crafts was part of her service to the community during her time there. This work was notable enough to be covered in a newspaper article.

explicit_text: Among the traditional items my mother taught Korean farmers to make was waraji, which are straw sandals. She was very capable and shared her knowledge of making waraji, goza, mino, and mushiro during her three years in Korea. Her efforts were appreciated and documented in a newspaper article.
implicit_text: Among the traditional items my mother taught farmers in that East Asian country to make were straw sandals. She was very capable and shared her knowledge of making these sandals, woven straw mats, the straw raincoat, and bamboo drying tray during her three years there. Her efforts were appreciated and documented in a newspaper article.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Japan, where my family lived in a village with a store called Yokosa Mise. After the war, I returned to Japan and stayed for about 13 years before coming back here. I have many memories of my mother, father, and siblings in Japan, and I contributed to my village by donating to the school and building a memorial for the Akiyama family gravesite.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in the island nation of East Asia, where my family lived in a village with a store called Yokosa Mise. After the war, I returned to that country and stayed for about 13 years before coming back here. I have many memories of my mother, father, and siblings there, and I contributed to my village by donating to the school and building a memorial for the family gravesite.

explicit_text: When I got married, my husband and I left our hometown and went to Yokohama. We stayed in an inn there for about nine days before coming to America. I remember that my husband didn't have much money then and pretended to be drunk and sleepy so he wouldn't have to go out in town.
implicit_text: When I got married, my husband and I left our hometown and went to a major port city in East Asia. We stayed in an inn there for about nine days before coming to America. I remember that my husband didn't have much money then and pretended to be drunk and sleepy so he wouldn't have to go out in town.

explicit_text: At 18 years old, I went to Kobe to learn how to make men's garments for nine months because I felt there was no future in our village store business. My aunt lived there, and although my mother initially came to bring me back, I stayed to gain skills. This training helped me become a dressmaker before I got married.
implicit_text: At 18 years old, I went to a prominent port city in East Asia to learn how to make men's garments for nine months because I felt there was no future in our village store business. My aunt lived there, and although my mother initially came to bring me back, I stayed to gain skills. This training helped me become a dressmaker before I got married.

explicit_text: I married into the Akiyama family and took on responsibilities to honor them, especially after my father-in-law had a stroke. I contributed to building a large grave called Rekidai Bako for the Akiyama family, which cost about 20,000 yen. Even though others said the cost should be shared, I felt it was my duty since I was coming back to America and couldn't care for the grave personally.
implicit_text: I married into a family with the surname Akiyama and took on responsibilities to honor them, especially after my father-in-law had a stroke. I contributed to building a large grave called Rekidai Bako for that family, which cost about 20,000 yen. Even though others said the cost should be shared, I felt it was my duty since I was coming back to America and couldn't care for the grave personally.

explicit_text: Before I married into the Akiyama family, I saved over 800 yen from my dressmaking work and asked Mr. Kakuichi Harada, an honest man in our village, to hold the money for me. When I visited Japan after nine years, my father told me that Mr. Harada had to reveal the secret because the family was going through hard times. I was glad the money helped my family, and I appreciated Mr. Harada's honesty.

When I visited East Asia after nine years, my father told me that this man had to reveal the secret because the family was going through hard times. I was glad the money helped my family, and I appreciated his honesty.

explicit_text: I was a single woman until I was 21, which was considered old for marriage in my village where girls usually married at 16 or 17. People called me an 'old maid' because of this. Although my mother suggested I didn't need to go to America to marry, I eventually did, leaving home early and starting a new life with my husband.
